reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern that the zone ID passed to init_unavailable_range() may not match the actual zone spanning each PFN in memory holes between memblock regions, which can cause incorrect page flags and crashes. The author fixed this by looking up the correct zone for each PFN in the hole, which has no performance impact since it's init-only code running once at boot.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about hotplugged memory not being covered by superpageblocks, acknowledged the issue, and explained how they plan to fix it in a new function called resize_zone_superpageblocks(). This function will be called from move_pfn_range_to_zone() after the zone span has been updated. It will allocate a new superpageblock array with kvmalloc_node(), copy existing superpageblocks, and initialize new superpageblocks for the added range.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about memory fragmentation caused by stealing movable pageblocks for unmovable or reclaimable allocations, and responded by adding an asynchronous evacuation mechanism to migrate remaining movable pages out of the claimed block, allowing future unmovable/reclaimable allocations to be concentrated in fewer pageblocks.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about accurately tracking page contents in superpageblocks by extending the pageblock_data flags to include PB_has_unmovable, PB_has_reclaimable, and PB_has_movable bits. The new flags are set at allocation time and cleared on full pageblock free or when migration confirms no pages of that type remain. This change provides a foundation for superpageblock-level steering decisions.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about lock inversion in the PCP free path, acknowledged that using spin_trylock and falling back to free_one_page() defeats the per-CPU pageset benefits, and proposed a solution involving a per-CPU remote free list (llist) consumed by the owning CPU. The author agreed to implement this change.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the watermark_boost mechanism, which was causing excessive wakeups of kswapd due to sub-pageblock fallbacks and order-0 reclaim. The author agreed that this mechanism is not effective for superpageblocks and decided to drop it entirely, removing various related code paths.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the overhead of tracking superpageblocks, explaining that the array is allocated per-zone during boot via memblock and has negligible overhead (~48 bytes per superpageblock). The author confirmed that this is pure bookkeeping with no allocation behavior change.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about memory overhead and storage efficiency in the pageblock flags bitmap, agreed to replace it with a per-pageblock struct containing its own flags word, which eliminates bit-packing indexing and simplifies accessor code; no functional change is expected.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about locking contention on large machines, explained that the proposed solution extends the page cluster (PCP) to operate on whole pageblocks with ownership tracking, and confirmed that this change will eliminate affinity violations and shorten zone->lock hold time. The author also noted that the allocator degrades gracefully to traditional behavior when PCP batch sizes are small or the zone is fragmented.

reviews.author: Boris Burkov
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov pointed out that the patch's focus on cow and tree movement is misplaced, as extent_buffers with refs == 1 are already eligible for release_folio() and can be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim or drop_caches. He suggested that the actual issue is with valid, non-stale extent_buffers not currently in use by btrfs operations, which should be available for reclaim.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested a minor code improvement and provided a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that he has reviewed the patch.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested simplifying the patch by using GFP flags instead of a movable boolean, stating that this would allow callers to handle allocations correctly without additional documentation.
reviews.raw_body:
Small correction to a generally correct story:

every extent_buffer we have used (it is newly cowed or we have read it
for some reason) will settle into a state where extent_buffer->refs == 1
and it is eligible for release_folio(). If a block gets cowed, then its
extent_buffer should be fully released/freed right away, with a tricky
EXTENT_BUFFER_STALE bit dance to prevent accidental reuse during this
process.

So I don't think cow or trees moving around is all that relevant here.

The folios you are after are the ones which are sitting around with
extent_buffer->refs == 1 and which will return 0 to release_folio(),
which is any valid, non-stale extent_buffer no btrfs operation is
currently using (typically referred to from a btrfs_path). These are
already available to be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim,
drop_caches, etc.

---

I left a small code nit inline, but whether you take that suggestion or
leave it, you can add:

Reviewed-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Thanks for the patch,
Boris

---

My gut says it is nicer to just transparently pass along a gfp flag
rather than a carefully documented movable boolean and let the callers
do the right thing. Just a nit, I don't care strongly about it.

reviews.author: Rafael Wysocki
reviews.summary:
Rafael Wysocki suggested breaking out the code that updates the page_count value into a separate function, named do_mark_free_pages(), which would take list, buddy_list, and page_count as arguments.

Rafael Wysocki suggested a simplified version of the do_mark_free_pages() function call, replacing the existing logic with an if-else statement that checks if the zone has any superpageblocks before iterating over them.

2026-05-20.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the allocation of large memory blocks by dividing memory into superpageblocks and steering unmovable, reclaimable, and high-atomic allocations into those already tainted by such allocations. The goal is to leave as many 1GB superpageblocks available for movable allocations, which can be easily defragmented for regular or huge pages. Various strategies are employed to achieve this, including preferential allocation from tainted blocks, fallback to small pages for certain kernel allocations, and migration of movable memory when a block falls below a free threshold. This approach enables more extensive use of 2MB THPs and mTHPs, as well as reliable allocation of 1GB pages. The series is built on top of Johannes's PCPBuddy series, which aims to reduce the need for CPUs to get pages from zone free lists by relying on page block ownership.
